The Collection

Crystal Wine Glasses and Champagne Flutes

For the true connoisseur, enjoying a fine wine, champagne, cognac, or whisky begins long before the first sip. It starts with a ritual — the moment the hand takes hold of a perfectly balanced crystal glass, when light passes through the deep hand-cut facets, and every surface comes alive with countless sparkling reflections. At that moment, an ordinary drink becomes a refined ceremony.

The collection of crystal wine glasses and champagne flutes presented in this museum originates from the museum of the Pervomaysky Glass Factory and includes some of the finest examples of Soviet artistic crystal created during the 1970s and 1980s. These objects were never intended to be ordinary tableware. They were conceived as works of decorative art in which form, proportion, light, and craftsmanship were brought together in perfect harmony.

Each glass is the result of the combined work of a designer, a master glassblower, and a skilled crystal cutter. The elegant silhouette of the bowl, the refined stem, the solid foot, and the masterfully executed deep hand-cut decoration create a perfectly balanced composition in which every detail serves an artistic purpose. Because every piece was finished by hand, no two glasses are ever completely identical.

The artists of the Pervomaysky Glass Factory sought to create more than beautiful drinking vessels. They designed crystal that would enhance the experience of enjoying fine beverages. The exceptional clarity of the crystal and the brilliance of its deep-cut facets enrich the color of wine and champagne, while the satisfying weight and balance of each glass add a sense of elegance and ceremony to every occasion.

Today, these crystal glasses and champagne flutes are appreciated not only as functional objects but also as collectible works of twentieth-century decorative art. They demonstrate the remarkable achievements of the artists, master glassmakers, and crystal cutters of the Pervomaysky Glass Factory, whose craftsmanship represented one of the highest expressions of Soviet artistic glassmaking.

Each piece in this collection reflects a period when even the finest tableware was created with respect for beauty, traditional craftsmanship, and artistic excellence. Preserved today as museum objects, these crystal glasses are not simply historical artifacts — they are enduring works of art that continue to celebrate the legacy of one of the Soviet Union's most distinguished artistic glass factories.

Crystal goblet with diamond-cut trellis pattern and a scalloped foot — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Small faceted crystal vessel with vertical cut panels — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, museum collection.

Crystal wine glass with fan and leaf-motif cutting on a baluster stem — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

A crystal wine glass paired with a slender champagne flute, both hand-cut — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Crystal goblet with a radiating fan and star-cut design — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Crystal tumbler with a lower band of diamond and lattice cutting — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

A pair of crystal champagne flutes, each with a distinct hand-cut pattern — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Detail of a faceted stem with a hexagonal knop — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, museum collection.

A pair of crystal wine glasses — one with a swirling fan cut, the other with diamond and lattice cutting — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

A pair of crystal goblets with contrasting star-cut and square lattice-cut patterns — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

A matching pair of crystal glasses with diamond trellis cutting and faceted hexagonal stems — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.
